analogy: Think of "Legislature" through the chapter's own opening images: 100 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Five LEGISLATURE INTRODUCTION You have already studied the importance of elections and the method of election adopted in India. Legislatures are elected by the people and work on behalf of the people. In this chapter you would study how... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: LEGISLATURE, INTRODUCTION, WHY DO WE NEED A PARLIAMENT?, WHY DO WE NEED TWO HOUSES OF PARLIAMENT?. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Legislature is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 100 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Five LEGISLATURE INTRODUCTION You have already studied the importance of elections and the method of election adopted in India.

The text explains that legislatures are elected by the people and work on behalf of the people.

The text explains that in this chapter you would study how elected legislatures function and help in maintaining democratic government.

The text explains that you will also learn about the composition and functioning of the parliament and State legislatures in India and their importance in democratic government.

The text explains that after reading this chapter you would know ± the importance of the legislature; ± the functions and powers of the Parliament of India; ± the law making procedure; ± how the Parliament controls the executive; and ± how the Parliament regulates itself.

The text explains that 101 Chapter 5: Legislature WHY DO WE NEED A PARLIAMENT?

The text explains that lawmaking is but one of the functions of the legislature.

The text explains that it is the centre of all democratic political process.

The text explains that it is packed with action; walkouts, protests, demonstration, unanimity, concern and co-operation.

The text explains that indeed, a genuine democracy is inconceivable without a representative, efficient and effective legislature.

The text explains that the legislature also helps people in holding the representatives accountable.

The text explains that this is indeed, the very basis of representative democracy.

The text explains that yet, in most democracies, legislatures are losing central place to the executive.

The text explains that in India too, the Cabinet initiates policies, sets the agenda for governance and carries them through.

The text explains that this has led some critics to remark that the Parliament has declined.

The text explains that but even very strong cabinets must retain majority in the legislature.

The text explains that on account of its composition, it is the most representative of all organs of government.

The text explains that it is above all, vested with the power to choose and dismiss the government.

The text explains that 102 Indian Constitution at Work WHY DO WE NEED TWO HOUSES OF PARLIAMENT?

The text explains that the Constitution has given the States the option of establishing either a unicameral or bicameral legislature.

The text explains that the Finance Minister had to roll back the increases in fertilizer prices under intense opposition pressure (The Hindu, 12 March 2002) l On 4 June 1998, the Lok Sabha witnessed acrimonious scenes over the hike in urea and petroleum process.

The text explains that the issue rocked the house for two days leading to walkout by opposition.

The text explains that 103 Chapter 5: Legislature States having a bicameral legislature are given below: (i) Andhra Pradesh (ii) Bihar (iii) Karnataka (iv) Maharashtra (v) Telangana (vi) Uttar Pradesh

The text explains that 104 Indian Constitution at Work Countries with large size and much diversity usually prefer to have two houses of the national legislature to give representation to all sections in the society and to give representation to all geographical regions or parts of the country.

The text explains that the system of representation adopted for the Rajya Sabha is different from that in the USA.

The text explains that the number of members to be elected from each State has been fixed by the fourth schedule of the Constitution.

The text explains that what would happen if we were to follow the American system of equality of representation in the Rajya Sabha?

The text explains that the framers of the Constitution wanted to prevent such discrepancy.

The text explains that assembly is elected by a complex system combining direct and proportional representation for a period of four years.

The text explains that these members are generally the ministers in the governments at the state level and are appointed, not elected, by the governments of the federal states.

The text explains that according to the German law, all the members from one state have to vote as a bloc as per the instructions of the state governments.

The text explains that sometimes due to coalition government at the state level, they fail to reach an agreement and may have to abstain.

The text explains that 106 Indian Constitution at Work complete their term and elections are held for those one third seats only.

The text explains that lok Sabha The Lok Sabha and the State Legislative Assemblies are directly elected by the people.

The text explains that we have seen in the chapter on the executive that before the completion of five years, the Lok Sabha can be dissolved if no party or coalition can form the government or if the Prime Minister advises the President to dissolve the Lok Sabha and hold fresh elections.

The text explains that 107 Chapter 5: Legislature WHAT DOES THE PARLIAMENT DO?

The text explains that members other than ministers can also introduce bills but these have no chance of being passed without the support of the government. ± Control of Executive and ensuring its accountability: Perhaps the most vital function of the Parliament is to ensure that the executive does not overstep its authority and remains responsible to the people who have elected them.

The text explains that we shall discuss this function in greater detail later in this chapter. ± Financial Function: Government is about spending a lot of money on various matters.

The text explains that every Check your progress ± Do you think that composition of Rajya Sabha has protected the position of States of India? ± Should indirect election of Rajya Sabha be replaced by direct elections?

The text explains that 108 Indian Constitution at Work government raises resources through taxation.

The text explains that however, in a democracy, legislature controls taxation and the way in which money is used by the government.

The text explains that if the Government of India proposes to introduce any new tax, it has to get the approval of the Lok Sabha.

The text explains that the financial powers of the Parliament involve grant of resources to the government to implement its programmes.

The text explains that the government has to give an account to the legislature about the money it has spent and resources that it wishes to raise.

The text explains that the legislature also ensures that the government does not misspend or overspend.

The text explains that 109 Chapter 5: Legislature ± Constituent Function: The Parliament has the power of discussing and enacting changes to the Constitution.

The text explains that explain what would be the effect if these suggestions were accepted. √ Parliament should work for longer period. √ Attendance should be made compulsory for members of Parliament. √ Speakers should be empowered to penalise members for interrupting the proceedings of the House.

The text explains that arif wanted to know that if ministers propose most of the important bills and if the majority party often gets the government bills passed, what is the role of the Parliament in the law making process?

The text explains that which of the following statements you agree with the most?

The text explains that give your reasons. √ Legislators must be free to join any party they want. √ Anti-defection law has contributed to the domination of the party leaders over the legislators. √ Defection is always for selfish purposes and therefore, a legislator who wants to join another party must be disqualified from being a minister for the next two years.

The text explains that dolly and Sudha are debating about the efficiency and effectiveness of the Parliament in recent times.

The text explains that dolly believed that the decline of Indian Parliament is evident in the less time spent on debate and discussion and increase in the disturbances of the functioning of the House and walkouts etc.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work, Chapter 5: Legislature

Legislatures are elected by the people and work on behalf of the people. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch5 pg 1)
In this chapter you would study how elected legislatures function and help in maintaining democratic government. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch5 pg 1)

You will also learn about the composition and functioning of the parliament and State legislatures in India and their importance in democratic government. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch5 pg 1)

After reading this chapter you would know ± the importance of the legislature; ± the functions and powers of the Parliament of India; ± the law... (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch5 pg 1)
101 Chapter 5: Legislature WHY DO WE NEED A PARLIAMENT? (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch5 pg 1)
Lawmaking is but one of the functions of the legislature. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch5 pg 1)
It is packed with action; walkouts, protests, demonstration, unanimity, concern and co-operation. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch5 pg 1)
Indeed, a genuine democracy is inconceivable without a representative, efficient and effective legislature. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch5 pg 1)
The legislature also helps people in holding the representatives accountable. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch5 pg 1)

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
